summary: Gruber reflects on the Apple/Google no-poaching wage-fixing cartel scandal, highlighting newly unsealed documents showing Steve Jobs's role in the illegal agreements. He notes Eric Schmidt's naive attempt to avoid a paper trail while creating one via email. Gruber argues that Tim Cook would not have led Apple into this situation, either because he's too careful or because he would have recognized the arrangement as legally and morally wrong. The post positions this scandal as the worst aspect of Jobs's legacy, where his imperious nature crossed from asset to liability.
key_points: Newly unsealed court documents reveal the extent of Steve Jobs's involvement in the illegal no-poaching wage-fixing cartel with Google and other tech companies
Eric Schmidt tried to avoid a paper trail by suggesting verbal communication, while ironically creating an email trail saying exactly that
Gruber contends Tim Cook would not have participated in such agreements, either out of prudence or genuine moral objection
Jobs's imperious leadership style was both the source of Apple's greatest achievements and its most serious legal troubles
The tech industry has under-appreciated the ways Apple is actually better off without an imperious CEO
money_quotes: What strikes me is that I feel confident saying Apple wouldn't be in this trouble if Tim Cook had been in charge at the time.

If I want to be cynical, I'd say that's because Cook is too careful to put anything like this in writing. If I want to be idealistic, I'd say it's because Cook would not have agreed to participate in the agreement in the first place — that he'd have recognized that it was both legally and morally wrong.
This sort of imperious behavior is the worst aspect of Steve Jobs's legacy.
His hubris helped Apple achieve great things, but this is not the only time it landed the company in legal trouble.

For all the scrutiny Tim Cook has faced — and will continue to face, for the remainder of his career — regarding the numerous ways that Apple misses Steve Jobs, it's been under-remarked that in other ways, Apple is better served without an imperious CEO.
Seems bizarre to me that Schmidt wasn't aware that an email trail is just as incriminating as a paper trail — and more easily searched.
key_insight: Jobs's imperious nature was both the engine of Apple's greatness and the source of its worst legal and ethical missteps, and Apple is in some ways better served by a leader without that hubris.
